sections.title: 1 · Pronunciation (the silent-letter language)
bullets: Final consonants are usually SILENT: 'Paris' = pa-REE, 'petit' = puh-TEE.
But final consonants in the word CaReFuL (c, r, f, l) are often pronounced: 'sac', 'bonjour', 'neuf', 'mal'.
Nasal vowels: an/en = 'ahn', on = 'ohn', in/ain = 'an', un = 'uhn' — air goes through the nose, no hard N.
'r' is from the back of the throat (like a soft gargle). 'u' = round your lips for 'oo' but say 'ee'.
'ch' = sh, 'j'/'ge' = soft zh, 'gn' = ny (like canyon), 'qu' = k, final 'e' is usually silent.
Liaison: a silent final consonant links to the next vowel: 'les amis' = lay-zah-MEE.
sections.diagram:
SOUND   SPELLING        EXAMPLE
ah-n    an, en          dans, enfant
oh-n    on              bonjon, non
an      in, ain, ein    vin, pain, plein
oo-ee   ui              oui, huit
wah     oi              moi, trois
sh      ch              chat
zh      j, ge           je, manger

sections.title: 2 · Nouns have gender (le / la)
bullets: Every noun is masculine or feminine. Learn the article WITH the noun: not 'table' but 'la table'.
Definite (the): le (m), la (f), l' (before vowel), les (plural).
Indefinite (a/an): un (m), une (f), des (plural / some).
Plural usually adds -s (silent). The article is what you actually hear: le→les, un→des.
Rough hints: -tion, -té, -e are often feminine; -age, -ment, -eau often masculine (many exceptions).
sections.diagram:
MASC      FEM       PLURAL
the       le        la        les
a/some    un        une       des
example   le chat   la table  les chats

sections.title: 3 · Subject pronouns
bullets: je = I, tu = you (informal), il/elle/on = he/she/we(casual), nous = we, vous = you (formal/plural), ils/elles = they.
'on' is used constantly in speech to mean 'we': 'On y va' = Let's go.
'vous' is both polite-singular AND plural — use it with strangers and elders.
sections.diagram:
je      I
tu      you (informal)
il/elle he/she
on      we (casual) / one
nous    we
vous    you (formal/plural)
ils/elles  they (m/f)

sections.title: 4 · The 4 power verbs (present)
bullets: être (to be), avoir (to have), aller (to go), faire (to do/make) appear in a huge share of all sentences.
avoir is used for AGE and hunger: 'J'ai 20 ans' (I'm 20), 'J'ai faim' (I'm hungry).
aller builds the easy future: aller + infinitive = 'going to ...'.
sections.diagram:
ÊTRE    AVOIR   ALLER   FAIRE
je       suis    ai      vais    fais
tu       es      as      vas     fais
il/elle  est     a       va      fait
nous     sommes  avons   allons  faisons
vous     êtes    avez    allez   faites
ils      sont    ont     vont    font

sections.title: 5 · Regular -ER verbs (the biggest group)
bullets: ~90% of French verbs end in -ER and all conjugate the same way. Master one, master thousands.
Drop -er, add: -e, -es, -e, -ons, -ez, -ent. The -e, -es, -ent endings all sound identical (silent).
Examples: parler (speak), aimer (like/love), habiter (live), travailler (work), manger (eat).
sections.diagram:
PARLER (to speak)
je parle      nous parlons
tu parles     vous parlez
il parle      ils parlent
(parle / parles / parlent all sound the same)

sections.title: 6 · Negation: ne … pas
bullets: Wrap the verb: ne + VERB + pas. 'Je ne sais pas' = I don't know.
ne → n' before a vowel: 'Je n'aime pas' = I don't like.
In casual speech the 'ne' is often dropped: 'Je sais pas'.
Other pairs: ne…jamais (never), ne…rien (nothing), ne…plus (no more).

examples.target: Il n'y a pas de problème.

sections.title: 7 · Asking questions (3 ways)
bullets: Easiest: raise your intonation. 'Tu parles anglais ?' = You speak English?
Add 'Est-ce que' to the front: 'Est-ce que tu parles anglais ?'
Question words: qui (who), que/quoi (what), où (where), quand (when), pourquoi (why), comment (how), combien (how much).
Formal: invert verb+subject: 'Parlez-vous anglais ?'

sections.title: 8 · The past: passé composé
bullets: The everyday past tense = avoir (or être) in present + past participle.
Most verbs use avoir: 'J'ai mangé' (I ate / have eaten).
-ER verbs' participle ends in -é (mangé, parlé); -IR → -i (fini); -RE → -u (vendu).
A small set of movement/change verbs use être (aller, venir, partir, naître…): 'Je suis allé' — and the participle agrees: 'Elle est allée'.
sections.diagram:
avoir + participle:   J'ai parlé   (I spoke)
être  + participle:   Je suis allé(e) (I went)
DR & MRS VANDERTRAMP verbs take être

sections.title: 9 · The easy future: aller + infinitive
bullets: Futur proche = present of aller + a verb in the infinitive. Just like English 'going to'.
No new conjugation needed — you already know aller.
'Je vais manger' = I'm going to eat. 'On va voir' = We'll see.

examples.gloss: What are you going to do?

sections.title: 10 · Numbers, time & survival
bullets: 0-10: zéro, un, deux, trois, quatre, cinq, six, sept, huit, neuf, dix.
Watch out: 70 = soixante-dix (60+10), 80 = quatre-vingts (4×20), 90 = quatre-vingt-dix.
Politeness is mandatory: bonjour, s'il vous plaît, merci, pardon, excusez-moi.
Useful glue words: et (and), mais (but), parce que (because), donc (so), avec (with), pour (for).

examples.target: Bonjour, je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.
examples.gloss: Hello, I'd like a coffee, please.

examples.target: Je ne parle pas bien, mais j'apprends.
examples.gloss: I don't speak well, but I'm learning.
